content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of NNN positive integers. He can perform the following operation **at most once**:


Select an index iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) and an integer k≥1k\geq 1k≥1
Select kkk non-negative integers x1,x2,…,xkx_1, x_2,\ldots ,x_kx1​,x2​,…,xk​ such that 2x1+2x2+…+2xk=Ai2^{x_1} + 2^{x_2} + \ldots + 2^{x_k} = A_i2x1​+2x2​+…+2xk​=Ai​
Erase AiA_iAi​ from AAA and append 2x1,2x2,…,2xk2^{x_1}, 2^{x_2}, \ldots, 2^{x_k}2x1​,2x2​,…,2xk​ to AAA.
Note that the xix_ixi​ need not be distinct: for example it is allowed to write 8=21+22+218 = 2^1 + 2^2 + 2^18=21+22+21.

Chef wants to **maximize** the bitwise XOR of all the elements of the array after performing this operation **at most once**.

Can you help him find this maximum?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum possible XOR of the array after performing the mentioned operation at most once.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible bitwise XOR of the array after performing the described operation at most once.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We can select i=2i = 2i=2 and apply the operation. A2=7A_2 = 7A2​=7 can be represented as 1+1+1+41 + 1 + 1 + 41+1+1+4. So, we erase A2A_2A2​ and append 1,1,1,41, 1, 1, 41,1,1,4 to AAA.
Now, A=[5,7,1,1,1,4]A = [5, 7, 1, 1, 1, 4]A=[5,7,1,1,1,4], and the bitwise XOR of this array is 777 which is the maximum possible XOR.
Test case 333: In this case, it's beneficial to not apply the operation.
The bitwise XOR of A=[0,1,2,4,8,16]A = [0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16]A=[0,1,2,4,8,16] is 313131.

solution.approach:
For a chosen index i we erase Ai and replace it by any multiset of powers of two whose sum equals Ai. The XOR contributed by the replacement is the XOR of those powers, i.e. the XOR of all bit positions that appear an odd number of times. It can be shown that any integer R such that 0 ≤ R ≤ Ai and R has the same parity as Ai (R mod 2 = Ai mod 2) can be obtained as this XOR. Therefore, after fixing i, let X be the XOR of all elements except Ai. We have to choose R in the allowed set to maximise X xor R. Setting Z = R, the problem becomes: maximise X xor Z subject to 0 ≤ Z ≤ Ai and Z mod 2 = Ai mod 2.

We solve this with a digit‑DP on the binary representation (up to 60 bits). The DP state is (position, tight, parity) where:
- position: current bit (from most significant to 0).
- tight: whether the already built prefix of Z is exactly equal to the prefix of Ai (1) or already smaller (0).
- parity: parity of the number of 1‑bits placed in Z so far.
Transition tries Z_bit = 0 or 1 respecting the tight constraint, updates tight and parity, and adds the contribution (X_bit xor Z_bit) << position to the answer. The base case (position = -1) is valid only if parity equals required parity (Ai mod 2).
The DP runs in O(60·4) per index, which is fast enough for N up to 2·10^5 (≈12 million operations). The answer for a test case is the maximum value obtained over all i, and the original XOR (no operation) is automatically considered because Z = Ai is a feasible choice.

Complexities:
- Time: O(N·B) where B = 60 (bits of Ai).
- Memory: O(1) per DP (a few small arrays).
